corrections.note: Milestones (1958) was released in the US on Columbia (CL 1193 / CS 1193). 'CBS' was only the international/parent branding; for a US-catalog the correct label is Columbia. The album's own DNA prose already says Columbia.
Miles Smiles (1967) was released on Columbia (CL 2601 mono / CS 9401 stereo), recorded Oct 24-25 1966 at Columbia 30th Street Studio. Confirmed via Wikipedia infobox, milesdavis.com, and Discogs.
Such Sweet Thunder (1957) was originally released on Columbia (CL 1033). Confirmed via Columbia University news page, Wikipedia infobox, and Discogs original-pressing listings.

corrections.current: Alongside Coltrane on tenor saxophone, the album features McCoy Tyner (piano), Jimmy Garrison and Elvin Jones (drums) playing original Coltrane compositions.
corrections.corrected: Alongside Coltrane on tenor saxophone, the album features McCoy Tyner (piano), Jimmy Garrison (bass) and Elvin Jones (drums) playing original Coltrane compositions.
corrections.note: Personnel error: the prose groups Jimmy Garrison with Elvin Jones as '(drums)'. Garrison is the bassist (double bass); only Elvin Jones plays drums. Wikipedia infobox: 'Jimmy Garrison - double bass; Elvin Jones - drums.'

corrections.current: Launched the jazz fusion movement and became the first jazz album to sell 500,000 copies in its first year.
corrections.corrected: Launched the jazz fusion movement and became Miles Davis's first gold record (RIAA Gold 1976, Platinum 2003), selling roughly 400,000 copies in its first year.
corrections.note: The superlative 'first jazz album to sell 500,000 copies in its first year' is unsupported. Sources cite ~400,000 first-year sales; RIAA Gold (500,000) certification came in 1976, six years after release, and was Davis's first gold record (not a jazz-wide first). Platinum in 2003. RIAA database: certification dated 2003-09-22.

corrections.current: The title track's riff was later sampled by Steely Dan for 'Rikki Don't Lose That Number.'
corrections.corrected: The title track's bass intro was later borrowed by Steely Dan for 'Rikki Don't Lose That Number' (an interpolation Donald Fagen has disputed, calling it a generic Brazilian bass line).
corrections.note: Not a sample. Steely Dan's 1974 track borrows/interpolates the unison piano-and-bass intro figure as played by the band, not a digital sample; and the borrowing is disputed by Donald Fagen, who denied the similarity. 'Sampled' is the wrong term.

corrections.corrected: A makossa-funk landmark and one of the most influential African records in global pop.
corrections.note: Genre error. 'Soul Makossa' (1972) is makossa/soul-funk (Discogs tags: Funk/Soul, makossa), not Afrobeat. Afrobeat is the specific Nigerian genre pioneered by Fela Kuti and Tony Allen; Dibango was Cameroonian and his signature genre is makossa. Crediting him with pioneering Afrobeat 'before Fela' conflates two distinct genres.

corrections.current: This was Coltrane's first album as leader for the label, with which he had signed a new contract the previous year.
corrections.corrected: This was Coltrane's first album as sole leader for the label, with which he had signed a new contract the previous year.
corrections.note: Giant Steps was NOT Coltrane's first Atlantic album as leader: 'Bags & Trane' (co-led with Milt Jackson, recorded Aug 1959, released 1959) preceded it. Giant Steps was his first album as SOLE leader for Atlantic. Wax Poetics: it 'wasn't even his debut Atlantic album, the first being 1959's Bags & Trane.'

corrections.current: It was the first album to feature Coltrane playing soprano saxophone.
corrections.corrected: It was the first released album to feature Coltrane playing soprano saxophone.
corrections.note: Coltrane's earliest soprano-saxophone recording was the Don Cherry session of June 28, 1960 (released later, in 1966, on 'The Avant-Garde'), which predates the My Favorite Things sessions (Oct 1960). As a statement about the first RELEASED album with his soprano it is defensible; as worded ('first album to feature') it is misleading. Adding 'released' makes it accurate.

corrections.corrected: Django Reinhardt was the first major jazz voice to emerge from Europe.
corrections.note: artists.json bio. 'Invented European jazz' is an overstatement no reputable source supports; jazz was already played across Europe in the 1920s before Reinhardt rose. Sources frame him as 'one of the first major jazz talents to emerge in Europe' / the first important European jazz guitarist (Wikipedia, Britannica). NOTE: this correction targets src/data/artists.json (artistId django-reinhardt), not albums.json.
